primary_diagnosis.name: Open displaced fracture of the left tibial shaft

pathophysiology_summary: A high-energy fall applied a bending load to the tibia that exceeded cortical strength, producing a displaced mid-shaft fracture. The sharp fracture ends breached the overlying skin, creating an OPEN (compound) fracture: the fracture haematoma now communicates with the environment, so the bacterial contamination of the wound places the patient at high risk of deep infection (osteomyelitis) unless it is surgically debrided and washed out and antibiotic prophylaxis is given within hours. The tibia's subcutaneous anterior surface and tight anterior/deep-posterior compartments mean the injury also threatens the neurovascular structures: the posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis supply, the deep peroneal/tibial nerves, and — because bleeding and oedema into a closed fascial space rises over the first hours — acute compartment syndrome. At presentation distal pulses, capillary refill and sensation are intact, but this is a snapshot; the risk peaks as swelling develops. Definitive care is emergency orthopaedic: analgesia, wound cover, realignment/traction splinting, IV antibiotics, tetanus prophylaxis and operative debridement plus fixation.

differential.diagnosis: Open displaced fracture of the tibia
differential.evidence_basis: Audible crack + gross angular deformity + a wound with visible bone through the skin + complete loss of weight-bearing after a high-energy fall.

differential.diagnosis: Severe soft-tissue injury / ligamentous sprain of the lower leg
differential.evidence_basis: Visible angular deformity, protruding bone and bony crepitus confirm a structural cortical break, not a ligamentous injury.

differential.diagnosis: Acute compartment syndrome of the leg
differential.evidence_basis: A displaced tibial fracture is the commonest cause; currently excluded on intact pulses/sensation/capillary refill but risk rises sharply over the coming hours.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening picture the fracture is near-certain; the reasoning work is recognising the OPEN wound (surgical emergency) and holding compartment syndrome and vascular injury open as time-dependent threats.

differential.diagnosis: Open displaced fracture of the tibia
differential.evidence_basis: Open wound over the fracture confirmed; mechanism high-energy; complete functional loss.
High-energy mechanism sufficient to break healthy bone and no prior malignancy or bone-disease history.
Displaced sharp fragments can lacerate the tibial artery, especially during unsplinted movement/transport; distally intact now but must be protected and monitored.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The open wound and the mechanism close out the benign mimics; the live differentials become the COMPLICATIONS (compartment syndrome, vascular/nerve injury, osteomyelitis).

differential.diagnosis: Open (compound) displaced fracture of the left tibial shaft with intact distal neurovascular status at presentation
differential.evidence_basis: Diagnostic tetrad of an open displaced fracture; the management priority is time-critical emergency orthopaedic care and prevention of neurovascular and infective complications.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A confident telehealth working diagnosis of an open, displaced long-bone fracture; imaging (plain radiographs) confirms cortical disruption and displacement but must not delay 000 escalation.

red_flags.description: Open wound over the fracture with bone visible through the skin — this is an OPEN (compound) fracture
red_flags.clinical_significance: Converts a closed fracture into a surgical emergency: high risk of deep bone infection (osteomyelitis). Requires emergency debridement/washout, IV antibiotics and tetanus prophylaxis within hours.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed antibiotics and washout markedly increase the risk of chronic osteomyelitis, non-union and possible limb loss.

red_flags.description: Gross angular deformity with complete loss of function of the limb (cannot bear weight or move the ankle)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates a displaced fracture requiring realignment and traction splinting; unsplinted movement risks further soft-tissue and neurovascular damage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Movement of unsplinted displaced fragments can lacerate vessels/nerves and worsen the injury during transport.

red_flags.description: Impending acute compartment syndrome — displaced tibial fracture with rising swelling over the coming hours
red_flags.clinical_significance: Compartment syndrome is a limb-threatening emergency; the earliest sign is pain out of proportion / on passive stretch, followed later by paraesthesia and a tense swollen compartment. Currently absent but the risk peaks in the first hours.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed compartment syndrome causes irreversible muscle/nerve necrosis, contracture and possible amputation.

red_flags.description: Neurovascular compromise of the foot — the foot becoming pale/blue, cold, numb or pulseless
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distal pulses, capillary refill and sensation are intact at presentation (pertinent negatives), but a distally-checked foot that changes colour/temperature/sensation signals arterial injury or compartment syndrome and mandates immediate escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised arterial injury or compartment syndrome leads to limb ischaemia and loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising the patient to get a lift or drive to ED (T4) rather than calling an ambulance — an unsplinted displaced open fracture can suffer vascular injury in transit; traction splinting by paramedics is part of the treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attempting to realign or reduce the limb
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Telling the patient or carer to straighten the leg or push the protruding bone back in — this risks lacerating the tibial artery/nerve and drives contamination deeper. The limb is immobilised in the position found.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from intact distal pulses
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating currently-normal pulses, capillary refill and sensation as a reason to relax — compartment syndrome and swelling evolve over hours, so the foot must be monitored and re-checked.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the open wound as the key upgrade
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Managing it as a routine closed fracture and not appreciating that the skin breach makes it a time-critical surgical emergency needing antibiotics and washout within hours.
Offering food, drink or oral pain relief — the patient is nil-by-mouth for likely same-day operative washout/fixation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of bony crepitus and the exact fracture pattern
Clinician assessment of distal pulses (dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial), capillary refill and light-touch sensation
Blood pressure, heart rate and other clinician-measured vitals
Plain radiographs demonstrating cortical disruption and displacement
Wound depth, degree of contamination and true soft-tissue injury (Gustilo–Anderson grading)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance (Priority 1) for parenteral analgesia, traction splinting, wound cover and transfer to a trauma/orthopaedic centre. Instruct only carer-deliverable first aid meanwhile: do not move or straighten the limb, cover the wound with a clean/sterile dressing without touching the bone, keep the patient still and nil-by-mouth, and monitor the foot's colour/warmth/sensation.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the fracture itself (the constellation is unmistakable). The real risk is UNDER-action — advising self-transport instead of an ambulance, or instructing realignment of the limb — and failing to recognise the open wound as a surgical emergency.

guidelines_referenced.reference: BOAST / BAPRAS Open Fractures of the Lower Limb
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Open fractures require urgent IV antibiotics, wound cover, realignment and splinting, tetanus prophylaxis and timely surgical debridement; do not repeatedly handle or irrigate the wound in the field.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG) — Antibiotic prophylaxis for open fractures
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Give antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g. a first-generation cephalosporin such as cefazolin) as soon as possible after an open fracture, with tetanus status review.
